item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wrote a story about this object for the Wanderer's Library a while ago. Can I link to it from the article, or is crosslinking between SCP and the Library not allowed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's better to show, not tell. That's tricky (and the original version of this did a lot more telling), but the reader's imagination is always scarier than anything I could write. Failing to do that is one of the many problems with my SCP-4314.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like this article. It doesn't attempt to explain the entirety of the backstory, but gives an amusing tale and has great writing for what its trying to do. It doesn't try hard to explain the cave as well, just this weird dude who eats himself. Its perfect
+1 from me for sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, good point. I would use some Indonesian slang if I actually knew anything whatsoever about the language, but for now I'll settle for getting rid of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got to agree, I think Addendum 4820-3 was a satisfying end to this; there weren't any kickers in the rest of the article to compete with the "oh shit, he's using his own body parts for all his survival needs!"

It bothers me that the interview dialogue, supposedly translated from Indonesian, reads to me just like a native English speaker. I'm not familiar with Indonesian specifically, and its colloquialisms and abbreviations, but words/phrases like "gonna", "Oh, well", "more-or-less", as a few examples, sound too English-specific to me to come from a translated conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Implied Sarkic tooth fairy! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck yeah. This keeps you reading, it’s interesting, and it’s got such a cool coolness

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Survivor Type" by Stephen King. One of the gems from his nasty, economical short story period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember seeing this article a few days ago with the vote counter in the negatives, but it looks like you've turned it around. Great job, and great SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a mix between SCP-3240 and a certain story I read about a castaway who ate himself while nuking his nerves on cocaine or something like that. And it's honestly pretty good.

I'm upvoting this not only because it's competently written but also because you successfully took some rather mundane elements to tell a great tale. Taking out the Sarkic bits and leaving the article with the monologue is definitely one hell of a hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
generally speaking, humanoid anomalies are not usually referred to as "objects". You can still use "subject", "entity", "SCP", "humanoid", and the like, depending on context.

  • Referring to humanoids as "objects" is fine, especially when you're going for an unsettling tone.
  • I think "instance" is generally the most accurate and professional term for the Foundation to use; in-universe, this file is about the procedures, and the person in the article is only relevant as an instance of an organism on which said procedures are to be used (even though they're often the only known instance).
  • The most realistic and humane term is probably "individual," which is how IRL behavior management documents refer to the people they're designed for.
  • "subject" is only appropriate to use in the context of a specific experiment, and generally not accurate as a label for a contained person.
  • In-universe documents should never refer to an anomalous person or thing as an "SCP" - SCP is an acronym for Special Containment Procedures. While it makes sense that personnel would refer to anomalous things as SCPs or skips just like we do on the site, they wouldn't do so in official documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, I was going to suggest that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They tested the living hell out of it first. Maybe I should state that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did use the sandbox, and all of 1 person reviewed it while it was in the forums.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Relatively minor complaint, but generally speaking, humanoid anomalies are not usually referred to as "objects". You can still use "subject", "entity", "SCP", "humanoid", and the like, depending on context. It's clear you've put a lot of effort into this one. It's not entirely new or revolutionary, but it's good enough that it doesn't deserve to be deleted or anything like that.

I'm glad that you're taking the time to edit this. Most authors (myself included) tend to have a "fire and forget" policy on publishing SCPs, so the fact that you're making major improvements is not only motivating, it's inspiring.

That said, you should probably use the sandbox first next time.

Still, good job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good shit. I'm a little put off by the idea that the Foundation would so readily use donor blood and organs from an anomalous human, but not enough to ruin the article for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The edits made a big difference, especially that final monologue, and I've gone from 0 to +1 on this. Nice job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was skeptical. It's the usual body-healing-immortal-sentient SCP. But, after reaching the end, I kinda like it. The fact that he treats the self-cannibalism as a ritual might be why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah the Sarkicism didn’t really add much. So I removed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this. I love the basic idea here, but I think it goes on a little too long, and that more about sarkicism at the end really hurts it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would be stronger if it ended with the third addendum. That list of items made a big impact, but nothing after that added anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how you've used a common ability, but put a new twist on it to make is interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can dig the body horror here, the note at the end absolutely kills it for me. It's not well-handled; what would have been a better idea would have been to just end it at the interview and leave a cross-link to the Sarkicism hub somewhere in there, so that the readers can get the implication for themselves.

E: Now that it's been edited, happy to give it a +1.
